author: Yangda Peng, Yunjia Qi, Hao Peng, Haotian Xia et al.

readme.entry: - **[Can LLM-as-a-Judge Reliably Verify Rubrics in Agentic Scenarios?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29920v1)** — Yangda Peng, Yunjia Qi, Hao Peng, Haotian Xia et al. — <https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.29920v1> · *paper/dataset/code* — RuVerBench meta-evaluates LLM-as-judge rubric verification on deep-research and agentic-coding outputs. 🆕

factuality.reason: structured evidence plus fetched source snapshot

quality.reason: concrete eval artifacts or methodology signals present

recency.reason: evidence indicates 2025-2026 or actively updated source

section: 8 · LLM-as-judge & verifiers (alignment, biases, verifiable vs judgeable)
title: Can LLM-as-a-Judge Reliably Verify Rubrics in Agentic Scenarios?

worker.candidate_reason: It tests the reliability of rubric verification in agentic settings rather than assuming judge scores are trustworthy, which is central to this repo’s verifier guidance.
worker.evidence: arXiv metadata published 2026-06-29 in cs.CL | authors: Yangda Peng, Yunjia Qi, Hao Peng, Haotian Xia, Guanzhong He et al. | abstract evidence: Rubric-based scoring has become a widely used paradigm in model evaluation, typically with LLM-as-a-Judge (LaaJ) for rubric scoring. | abstract names RuVerBench with 2,458 human-labeled rubric-verification instances and dataset/code at github.com/THU-KEG/RuVerBench. | URL reachability used the arXiv API alternate link with explicit v1 because the versionless page returned 404 during vetting.
worker.summary: RuVerBench meta-evaluates LLM-as-judge rubric verification on deep-research and agentic-coding outputs.
